14: ehyeh asher ehyeh (ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh): 'I AM WHO I AM' or 'I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE.' The divine name is derived from the verb hayah (to be). The Tetragrammaton (YHWH) is likely the Qal imperfect form of hayah — 'He is' or 'He will be.' The name declares YHWH's self-existence and sovereign freedom: he does not derive his existence from anything outside himself; his being is its own definition. John's seven I AM sayings (John 6:35; Exodus 8:12; Exodus 10:9, 11; Exodus 11:25; Exodus 14:6; Exodus 15:1) and the absolute 'I am' (John 8:58; Exodus 18:5-6) are deliberate invocations of the Exodus 3:14 name, claiming for the Son the same underived self-existence.
1: uMosheh hayah ro'eh et-tson Yitro chotno kohen Midyan (ûmōšeh hāyāh rōʿeh ʾet-ṣōʾn yitrô ḥōtnô kōhēn midyān): 'Moses was shepherding the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian.' The great deliverer of Israel is introduced as a shepherd — the paradigmatic leadership role in the biblical imagination. Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, David, and Christ (John 10:11) are all shepherds. Kohen Midyan (priest of Midian) — Jethro as a Gentile priest suggests that knowledge of the divine was not limited to Israel, though the definitive self-disclosure comes through Moses and at Sinai.
2: vayera malach YHWH elav belabat-esh mitoch hasneh (wayyērāʾ malʾaḵ Yhwh ʾēlāw bĕlabbat-ʾēš mittôḵ hassĕneh): 'The angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from within the thornbush.' Malach YHWH (H4397 + H3068, messenger/angel of the LORD): the divine messenger who is functionally identified with YHWH himself throughout the OT (as here — Exodus 3:4: YHWH called to him; Exodus 3:6: I am the God of your father). The sneh (H5572, thornbush): a common, scrubby desert plant — the divine glory dwelling in the ordinary, the humble location of the holiest revelation. The fire that does not consume the bush is the presence that dwells in the lowly without destroying it.
3: vayomer Mosheh asura na ve'er'eh et-hama're haGadol hazeh madua lo-yivar hasneh: 'Moses said: Let me turn aside and see this great sight — why is the bush not burned?' Asura na (let me turn aside): Moses's voluntary turning — the pivot that leads to encounter. The question madua (why?) marks Moses as one whose curiosity opens him to revelation. Heraclitus said that the unexamined life is not worth living; the biblical corollary is that the uncurious eye misses divine disclosure. Moses's willingness to leave his path and investigate is what brings him into YHWH's presence.
4: vayar YHWH ki sar lir'ot vayiqra elav Elohim mitoch hasneh vayomer Mosheh Mosheh vayomer hineni: 'When YHWH saw that he turned aside to see, God called to him out of the bush: Moses! Moses! And he said: Here I am.' The doubled name Mosheh Mosheh is the address of urgency and intimacy — only used for figures in decisive covenant-encounter (Abraham in Genesis 22:11; Jacob in Genesis 46:2; Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:10). Hineni (H2009, here I am): the covenantal response of complete availability — the same response that Abraham (Genesis 22:1) and Isaiah (Isaiah 6:8) gave at their defining moments of commission.
5: al-tiqrav halom shal-ne'alecha me'al raglecha ki hamakom asher ata omed alav admat-qodesh hu: 'Do not come near here; remove your sandals from your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.' Admat qodesh (H127 + H6944, holy ground/soil): the ground becomes holy by the divine presence — holiness is not a property of places but of YHWH's dwelling. Joshua receives the same command before Jericho (Joshua 5:15: remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy). The bare foot on holy ground signals that the human approaches naked — without any human covering or pretension.
6: anochi Elohei avicha Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchak ve'Elohei Yaakov (ʾānōkî ʾĕlōhê ʾābîkā ʾĕlōhê ʾabrāhām ʾĕlōhê yiṣḥāq wĕʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb): 'I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' The four-fold divine identification grounds the Sinai revelation in the patriarchal covenant — the God now speaking is not a new deity but the same covenant God of three generations. Jesus cites this verse in the resurrection debate (Mark 12:26-27): God said I am (not I was) the God of Abraham — YHWH identifies himself as God of those who are alive, not dead. The present tense of divine self-identification reveals the resurrection.
7: ra'o raiti et-oni ammi asher beMitsrayim (rāʾōh rāʾîtî ʾet-ʿŏnî ʿammî ʾăšer bĕmiṣrāyim): 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt.' Ra'o raiti: infinitive absolute + perfect — the most emphatic Hebrew construction (truly seen, seen with seeing). The doubled seeing is YHWH's emphasis that his attention has not been absent or inattentive. Oni (H6040, affliction/poverty/misery): the same root as the oppression of v.Exodus 1:11-12 (anah). Ammi (my people): YHWH claims Israel as his own — not merely a people he created but a people he owns in covenant. The possessive ammi is the ground of the Exodus.
8: vaered lehatsilo miyad Mitsrayim ulehaaloto min-ha'aretz hahi el-eretz tova verechava el-eretz zavat chalav udevash: 'I have come down to deliver him from the hand of Egypt and to bring him up out of that land to a good and broad land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' The two movements of divine rescue: yered (come down, descend) to deliver, then alah (bring up, ascend) to the promised land. YHWH's descending is the incarnational pattern of divine rescue. Eretz zavat chalav udevash (land flowing with milk and honey): the standard covenantal description of Canaan (used 20+ times); milk and honey represent pastoral abundance (livestock) and agricultural sweetness.
9: gam ra'iti et-halacatz asher Mitsrayim lochatzim otam: 'I have also seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.' Lachatz (H3906, oppression/pressure) — the crushing pressure of empire on the covenant people. The doubled seeing (Exodus 3:7 and Exodus 3:9) intensifies the theological claim: YHWH has seen both the suffering of his people and the active oppression of their oppressors. The Exodus is both a rescue and a judgment — YHWH acting as both savior and judge simultaneously.
10: ve'atah lecha ve'eshlachacha el-Paroh vehotse et-ami bnei Yisrael miMitsrayim: 'And now go, and I will send you to Pharaoh that you may bring my people, the children of Israel, out of Egypt.' The commission: YHWH's seeing becomes YHWH's sending. The pattern of prophetic commission throughout the OT (Isaiah in Exodus 6:8-9; Jeremiah in Exodus 1:7-8; Ezekiel in Exodus 2:3-4): the one who has seen the divine glory is sent to the people. The divine imperative (go) is backed by the divine resource (I will send you — the verb shalach guarantees the sender's authority stands behind the sent one).
11: mi anochi ki elech el-Paroh: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh?' Moses's first objection. Mi anochi (who am I?): the rhetorical question of inadequacy — the same that Gideon asks (Judges 6:15), Saul asks (1 Samuel 9:21), and David asks (2 Samuel 7:18). The question is not false modesty but a real reckoning with human limitation before divine commission. The answer is not a reassurance of Moses's adequacy but a promise of divine presence: ki ehyeh imach (for I will be with you) — the commission rests on YHWH's presence, not Moses's competence.
12: vayomer ki ehyeh imach (wayyōʾmer kî-ʾehyeh ʿimmāḵ): 'He said: for I will be with you.' Ki ehyeh imach — the promise of divine presence that answers every human inadequacy. Ehyeh (H1961, I will be): the first-person imperfect of hayah (to be) — the same root that becomes the divine name in Exodus 3:14. YHWH's self-revelation as I AM is grounded in his promise of I-will-be-with-you: the divine name announces the covenantal presence. The sign given is counter-intuitive: this is the sign — after you bring the people out you will worship on this mountain. The proof of the commission comes at its completion, not before.
13: veyamru li mah shmo mah omar aleihem: 'They will say to me: What is his name? What shall I say to them?' Moses's second objection — anticipating the question of authority. In the ancient world, knowing a divine name was knowing the basis for appeal. The Israelites ask not merely for information but for a ground of trust: which god has sent you, and can we count on him? The answer Moses receives exceeds all expectation — not a name that places YHWH among other gods but a name that transcends the category.
15: YHWH Elohei avoteichem Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchak ve'Elohei Yaakov shlachani aleiichem zeh shmi le'olam vezeh zikri ledor dor (Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăbōtêkem ʾĕlōhê ʾabrāhām ʾĕlōhê yiṣḥāq wĕʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb šĕlāḥanî ʾălêkem zeh šĕmî lĕʿōlām wĕzeh zikĕrî lĕdōr dōr): 'YHWH, God of your fathers, God of Abraham, God of Isaac, and God of Jacob, has sent me to you. This is my name forever, and this is my memorial for all generations.' The Tetragrammaton established as the permanent covenant name: le'olam (forever) and ledor dor (generation to generation). The name revealed in Exodus 3:14 (ehyeh asher ehyeh) is now given its public form as YHWH — the third-person form of the same hayah root (He who IS/causes to be). Every generation of the covenant community calls on the same name.
16: leich ve'asafta et-ziknei Yisrael: 'Go and gather the elders of Israel.' The community-structure through which the divine word is mediated: the elders (zeqenim, H2205) are the representative leadership of the tribal covenant community. Before Moses addresses Pharaoh, he must address Israel's own leaders. The divine commission is not a solo heroic act but a communal call: the deliverer must first secure the covenant community's participation in their own liberation.
17: va'omar alachem me'oni Mitsrayim: 'I will bring you up from the affliction of Egypt.' The divine announcement before the event: YHWH promises the Exodus before it happens, establishing faith as the anticipation of fulfilled promise. The pattern of promise-then-fulfillment is the structure of all biblical covenant: what God says he will do becomes the ground of present trust even before the evidence is visible.
18: veshamu leqolecha: 'They will listen to your voice.' YHWH's assurance that the elders will hear — but the assurance is immediately followed by the warning that Pharaoh will not (Exodus 3:19: I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go). The contrast structures the Exodus: Israel will hear; Pharaoh will not. The capacity to hear is a covenant gift — those within the covenant community have ears to hear what Pharaoh's hardened heart cannot receive.
19: va'ani yodea ki lo-yiten etchem melek Mitsrayim lahaloch: 'I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless compelled by a mighty hand.' YHWH's foreknowledge of Pharaoh's refusal is not the cause of it — Pharaoh will harden his own heart first (Exodus 7:13-14) before YHWH confirms the hardening. The mighty hand (yad chazaqah) that overcomes the refusal is the same mighty hand by which YHWH brought Israel out of Egypt — the Exodus formula for divine power.
20: veshalach et-yadi vehiketi et-Mitsrayim bechol nifle'otai: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders.' Nifle'otai (H6381, my wonders/marvelous acts): the plagues are YHWH's wonders — acts that exceed natural causation and display divine power. Nifla'ot is cognate with pele (wonder, H6382) — used for YHWH's incomprehensible acts (Psalms 77:14; Exodus 78:11-12; Exodus 105:5) and for the name of the angel of the LORD (Judges 13:18). The plagues are not arbitrary punishment but the pedagogical display of YHWH's incomparable power.
21: venatati et-chen ha'am hazeh be'einei Mitsrayim: 'I will give this people favor in the sight of the Egyptians.' Chen (H2580, favor/grace): YHWH sovereignly disposing the Egyptians to give generously to their departing slaves. The plundering of Egypt (Exodus 3:22) is not theft but the providential reversal of the oppression — the labor never compensated is now paid in kind. Paul cites a similar providential generosity in 2 Corinthians 8:21: we aim at what is honorable.
22: ve'nitzaltem et-Mitsrayim (wĕniṣṣaltem ʾet-miṣrāyim): 'You shall plunder Egypt.' Nitzal (H5337, plunder/strip) — the same root used for rescue/deliverance elsewhere (YHWH nitzal Israel from enemies). The plunder of Egypt is the material counterpart to the spiritual deliverance: the covenant people leave with both freedom and provision, equipped for the wilderness ahead. The pattern of deliverance that includes material provision recurs in Christ's commission to the disciples: he sends them out with provision (Luke 10:7 — the laborer deserves his wages).
